attributes.stem:
A three-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a five-day history of fever, cough and difficulty breathing. He has had three previous hospital admissions for pneumonia, a liver abscess caused by Staphylococcus aureus, and a pulmonary Aspergillus infection in the past two years. He has no other medical conditions. His medications include trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and itraconazole for prophylaxis. His immunisations are up to date. His parent reports that his maternal uncle died in childhood from recurrent infections.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 130/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 40/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. There are coarse crackles throughout both lung fields. His chest X-ray shows bilateral consolidation with a rounded opacity in the right upper lobe.

Blood tests reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 105 g/L (110-140)
  • White cell count: 18.5 × 10⁹/L (4.5-13.5)
  • Neutrophils: 14.2 × 10⁹/L (1.5-8.5)
  • Platelets: 450 × 10⁹/L (150-400)
  • C-reactive protein: 180 mg/L (<5)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following tests is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with chronic granulomatous disease (CGD), an inherited immunodeficiency characterised by defective NADPH oxidase complex in phagocytes. As a result, patients are predisposed to catalase-positive bacterial and fungal infections (e.g., Staphylococcus aureus, Aspergillus spp.). The history of recurrent infections, particularly pneumonia and liver abscess, along with the family history suggests X-linked CGD, the most common form.

Neutrophil function studies is the gold standard for diagnosing CGD. The dihydrorhodamine (DHR) flow cytometry test or nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) test specifically assess the ability of neutrophils to generate an oxidative burst, which is defective in CGD. These tests directly measure the functional defect that defines the disease.

Lymphocyte subset analysis would be more appropriate for investigating T cell immunodeficiencies such as severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) or DiGeorge syndrome. CGD is a disorder of phagocyte function, not lymphocytes. While patients with T cell deficiencies can have recurrent infections, they typically present earlier in life with opportunistic infections, failure to thrive, and chronic diarrhoea. The normal growth and development between infections in this patient makes a T cell deficiency less likely.

Serum immunoglobulin levels are used to investigate antibody deficiencies such as common variable immunodeficiency or X-linked agammaglobulinaemia. These would be normal in CGD as it does not affect antibody production. Patients with antibody deficiencies typically present with recurrent sinopulmonary infections with encapsulated bacteria, rather than the deep-seated abscesses and fungal infections seen in CGD.

Complement function assay would be used to investigate complement deficiencies, which typically present with recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections (particularly Neisseria species) or autoimmune manifestations. Complement deficiencies do not predispose to the fungal infections or deep abscesses characteristic of CGD. Additionally, the elevated neutrophil count in this patient suggests an appropriate inflammatory response, which would be impaired in complement deficiency.

Vaccination responses are used to assess antibody production in response to specific antigens and would be normal in CGD as it does not affect antibody production. This test is more useful in investigating suspected antibody deficiency disorders. The fact that this patient is on antimicrobial prophylaxis and has a history of deep-seated infections rather than just recurrent upper respiratory tract infections makes an antibody deficiency unlikely.

Once CGD is diagnosed, genetic testing can be performed to identify the specific mutation, which is important for genetic counselling and determining the inheritance pattern (X-linked versus autosomal recessive).

Summary of Primary Immunodeficiencies

Immunodeficiency

Chronic granulomatous disease

NADPH oxidase (phagocyte function)

Catalase-positive bacteria, fungi, and deep abscesses

Opportunistic infections (e.g., mucocutaneous candidiasis), viral infections

Lymphocyte subsets

Encapsulated bacteria, sinopulmonary infections

Complement components

Neisseria species (especially terminal complement deficiency)

CH50/AH50

DiGeorge syndrome

T cells (thymic aplasia)

Viral, fungal infections

Lymphocyte subsets
learningPoints: Chronic granulomatous disease is diagnosed by demonstrating impaired neutrophil oxidative burst using dihydrorhodamine or nitroblue tetrazolium testing.
Chronic granulomatous disease is characterised by defective NADPH oxidase in phagocytes, leading to recurrent bacterial and fungal infections.
Patients with chronic granulomatous disease typically present with recurrent infections from catalase-positive organisms, particularly affecting the lungs, liver, and lymph nodes.
references: Rider NL, Jameson MB, Creech CB. Chronic Granulomatous Disease: Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, and Genetic Basis of Disease. J Pediatric Infect Dis Soc. 2018;7(suppl_1):S2-S5. doi:10.1093/jpids/piy008
McCusker C, Upton J, Warrington R. Primary immunodeficiency. Allergy Asthma Clin Immunol. 2018;14(Suppl 2):61. Published 2018 Sep 12. doi:10.1186/s13223-018-0290-5
Lee AY, Gray PE. Evaluating for immunodeficiency in children with recurrent infection. Aust Fam Physician. 2014;43(9):629-632.
